title: Apply Cavalieri's principle
description: Determine when it is appropriate to apply Cavalieri's principle and use it to justify why certain figures have equal volumes.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following figures all have the same height and same base area.



[[☃ image 1]]

**For which of the figures can we apply Cavalieri's principle to show they have the same volume?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can use Cavalieri's principle to show that two figures have the same volume if we establish:

- Their base areas are the same.
- They have the same cross-sectional area at any given point above their bases.
- Their heights are the same.

The question told us that the figures all have the same height and same base area. Do any of them have the same cross-sectional area at any given point above their bases?

[[☃ image 1]]

In each figure, the cross-sectional area is the base area, and this area stays the same at each height.

Since these cross-sectional areas are all equal, and the heights are all equal, we know that Cavalieri's principle applies. In other words, we know all three figures have the same volume.
Figures $A$, $B$, and $C$ have the same volume.

options.alt: A rectangular prism, an oblique rectangular prism, and a cylinder. The oblique shape leans to the right at the top.

choices.content: Only $A$ and $B$
Only $A$ and $C$
Only $B$ and $C$
$A$, $B$, and $C$

assessment_items.question_content:
The following figures all have the same height and same base area.



[[☃ image 1]]

**For which of the figures can we apply Cavalieri's principle to show they have the same volume?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can use Cavalieri's principle to show that two figures have the same volume if we establish:

- Their base areas are the same.
- They have the same cross-sectional area at any given point above their bases.
- Their heights are the same.

The question told us that the figures all have the same height and same base area. Do any of them have the same cross-sectional area at any given point above their bases?

[[☃ image 1]]

Their base areas are all equal. The cross-sectional areas of figures $\text{A}$ and $\text{B}$ stay the same at each height. However, the cross-sectional area of figure $\text{C}$ decreases as the height increases.

Since their heights are also equal, that means only figures $\text{A}$ and $\text{B}$ have the same volume.
Only figures $A$ and $B$ have the same volume.

options.alt: A rectangular prism, an oblique rectangular prism, and a rectangular pyramid. The oblique shape leans to the right at the top.

choices.content: Only $A$ and $B$
Only $A$ and $C$
Only $B$ and $C$
$A$, $B$, and $C$

assessment_items.question_content:
The following figures all have the same height and same base area.


[[☃ image 1]]

**For which of the figures can we apply Cavalieri's principle to show they have the same volume?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can use Cavalieri's principle to show that two figures have the same volume if we establish:

- Their base areas are the same.
- They have the same cross-sectional area at any given point above their bases.
- Their heights are the same.

The question told us that the figures all have the same height and same base area. Do any of them have the same cross-sectional area at any given point above their bases?

[[☃ image 1]]

Their base areas are all equal. The cross-sectional area of figure $\text{C}$ stays the same at each height. However, the cross-sectional areas in figures $\text{A}$ and $\text{B}$ decrease as the height increases.

We can think of the cross-sections of a pyramid-like solid as a collection of dilations of the base about the [[☃ definition 1]]. Their base areas are equal, so every layer in figure $\text{A}$ has a corresponding layer in $\text{B}$ with the same cross-sectional area. Since their heights are also equal, figures $\text{A}$ and $\text{B}$ have the same volume.
Only figures $A$ and $B$ have the same volume.

options.alt: A pyramid, an oblique cone, and an oblique cylinder. Both of the oblique shapes lean to the left at the top.

choices.content: Only $A$ and $B$
Only $A$ and $C$
Only $B$ and $C$
$A$, $B$, and $C$

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the following two prisms.



[[☃ image 1]]

Sandra tried to use Cavalieri's principle to show that the two figures have the same volume.

> *"The base areas are the same. Therefore, corresponding cross-sections have the same area. The prisms have the same height. So the volumes must be the same."*

**What is the first mistake Sandra made?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can use Cavalieri's principle to show that two figures have the same volume if we establish:

- Their base areas are the same.
- They have the same cross-sectional area at any given point above their bases.
- Their heights are the same.

Let's go through Sandra's work part by part and find the first mistake that she made.

###Base areas

> *"The base areas are the same."*

$\begin{align}
\text{area}_\text{A} &= \dfrac12bh
\\\\
&=\dfrac12 \cdot6  \cdot 7
\\\\
&=21
\\\\
\text{area}_\text{B} &= \dfrac12bh
\\\\
&=\dfrac12 \cdot 5 \cdot 8
\\\\
&=20
\end{align}$

Sandra is incorrect here.

Sandra said the base areas are the same, but **the base areas are not the same**.

(She made another mistake but this was her first mistake.)

options.alt: A triangular prism and an oblique triangular prism that leans to the right at the top. The triangular prism has a small base of six units, a small height of seven units, and a prism height of fourteen units. The base of the oblique triangular prism has a base of five units and a height of eight units. One of the slanted edges of the oblique triangular prism has a length of fifteen units, but the oblique prism has a height of fourteen units.

choices.content: The base areas are not the same.
It isn’t true that corresponding cross-sections have the same area.
The heights are not the same.

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the following rectangular-based and triangular-based prisms.



[[☃ image 1]]

Luke tried to use Cavalieri's principle to show that the two figures have the same volume.

> *"The base areas are the same. Therefore, corresponding cross-sections have the same area. The prisms have the same height. So the volumes must be the same."*

**What is the first mistake Luke made?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can use Cavalieri's principle to show that two figures have the same volume if we establish:

- Their base areas are the same.
- They have the same cross-sectional area at any given point above their bases.
- Their heights are the same.

Let's go through Luke's work part by part and find the first mistake that he made.

###Base areas

> *"The base areas are the same."*

$\begin{align}
\text{area}_\text{rectangle} &= bh
\\\\
&=6 \cdot 2
\\\\
&=12
\\\\
\text{area}_\text{triangle} &= \dfrac12bh
\\\\
&=\dfrac12 \cdot 6 \cdot 4
\\\\
&=12
\end{align}$

Luke is correct here.

###Cross-sectional areas

> *"...corresponding cross-sections have the same area."*



[[☃ image 1]]

In each figure, the cross-sectional area is the base area. We know the base areas are the same, and this area stays the same at each height, so Luke is correct here.

###Prism height

> *"The prisms have the same height."*

The height of a prism is the shortest distance from a point on one base to the plane of the other base. We always measure it at a right angle relative to the base.

The height of the rectangular prism is $10$ units, but the height of triangular prism is $9$ units. Luke is incorrect here.
Luke said the prisms have the same height, but **the heights are not the same**.

options.alt: A right rectangular prism and an oblique triangular prism that leans to the right at the top. The right rectangular prism has a length of six units, a width of two units, and a height of ten units. The base of the oblique triangular prism has a base of six units and a height of four units. One of the slanted edges of the oblique triangular prism has a length of ten, but the oblique prism has a height of nine.

choices.content: The base areas are not the same.
It isn’t true that corresponding cross-sections have the same area.
The heights are not the same.

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the following pyramids, whose bases are a square and an equilateral triangle.



[[☃ image 1]]

Kyra tried to use Cavalieri's principle to show that the two figures have the same volume.

> *"The base areas are the same. Therefore, corresponding cross-sections have the same area. So the volumes must be the same."*

**What is the first mistake Kyra made?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can use Cavalieri's principle to show that two figures have the same volume if we establish:

- Their base areas are the same.
- They have the same cross-sectional area at any given point above their bases.
- Their heights are the same.

Let's go through Kyra's work part by part and find the first mistake that she made.

###Base areas

> *"The base areas are the same."*

An equilateral triangle whose sides are all $3$ units long has a smaller area than a square who sides are all $3$ units long.



[[☃ image 1]]

Kyra is incorrect here.

Kyra said the base areas are the same, but **the base areas are not the same**.

(She made other mistakes, but this was her first mistake.)

options.alt: A square pyramid with a base side-length of three units and a slant height of eleven units. A triangular pyramid with base side lengths of three units and a slant height of eleven units.

choices.content: The base areas are not the same.
It isn’t true that corresponding cross-sections have the same area.
She did not establish that the heights are the same.

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the following cylinder and cone.



[[☃ image 1]]

Marcel tried to use Cavalieri's principle to show that the two figures have the same volume.

> *"The base areas are the same. Therefore, corresponding cross-sections have the same area. So the volumes must be the same."*

**What is the first mistake Marcel made?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can use Cavalieri's principle to show that two figures have the same volume if we establish:

- Their base areas are the same.
- They have the same cross-sectional area at any given point above their bases.
- Their heights are the same.

Let's go through Marcel's work part by part and find the first mistake that he made.

###Base areas

> *"The base areas are the same."*

The cylinder and the cone have congruent bases — a circle whose radius is $4$ units.

Marcel is correct here.

###Cross-sectional areas

> *"...corresponding cross-sections have the same area."*



[[☃ image 1]]

The cross-sectional area in the cylinder stays the same at each height. However, the cross-sectional area of the cone decreases as the height increases.

Marcel is incorrect here.

**Marcel said corresponding cross-sections have the same area, but that isn't true.**

(He made another mistake, but this was his first mistake.)

options.alt: A cylinder with a base radius of four units and a height of ten units. A cone with a base radius of four units and a height of ten units.

choices.content: The base areas are not the same.
It isn’t true that corresponding cross-sections have the same area.
He did not establish that the heights are the same.

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the following right pyramid and right prism. Both figures have a base that is a [[☃ definition 1]]  pentagon.


[[☃ image 1]]

Heidi tried to use Cavalieri's principle to show that the two figures have the same volume.

> *"The base areas are the same. Therefore, corresponding cross-sections have the same area. The figures have the same height. So the volumes must be the same."*

**What is the first mistake Heidi made?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can use Cavalieri's principle to show that two figures have the same volume if we establish:

- Their base areas are the same.
- They have the same cross-sectional area at any given point above their bases.
- Their heights are the same.

Let's go through Heidi's work part by part and find the first mistake that she made.

###Base areas

> *"The base areas are the same."*

The pyramid and the prism have congruent bases — a regular pentagon whose sides are all $1$ unit long.

Heidi is correct here.

###Cross-sectional areas

> *"...corresponding cross-sections have the same area."*


[[☃ image 1]]

The cross-sectional area of the prism stays the same at each height. However, the cross-sectional area of the pyramid decreases as the height increases.

Heidi is incorrect here.
**Heidi said corresponding cross-sections have the same area, but that isn't true.**

options.definition: Having sides that are all the same length and angles that are all the same measure

options.alt: A pentagonal pyramid with a vertical height of two units and a base side length of one unit. A pentagonal prism with a height of two units and a base side length of one unit.

choices.content: The base areas are not the same.
It isn’t true that corresponding cross-sections have the same area.
The heights are not the same.

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the following right pyramid and right prism. Both figures have a square base.



[[☃ image 1]]

Miguel tried to use Cavalieri's principle to show that the two figures have the same volume.

> *"The base areas are the same. Therefore, corresponding cross-sections have the same area. The figures have the same height. So the volumes must be the same."*

**What is the first mistake Miguel made?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can use Cavalieri's principle to show that two figures have the same volume if we establish:

- Their base areas are the same.
- They have the same cross-sectional area at any given point above their bases.
- Their heights are the same.

Let's go through Miguel's work part by part and find the first mistake that he made.

###Base areas

> *"The base areas are the same."*

The pyramid and the prism have congruent bases — a square whose sides are all $5$ units long.

Miguel is correct here.

###Cross-sectional areas

> *"...corresponding cross-sections have the same area."*



[[☃ image 1]]

The cross-sectional area of the prism stays the same at each height. However, the cross-sectional area of the pyramid decreases as the height increases.

Miguel is incorrect here.
**Miguel said corresponding cross-sections have the same area, but that isn't true.**

options.alt: A right square pyramid with a base side length of five units and a vertical height of ten units. A rectangular pyramid with a square base with a side length of five units and a height of ten units.

choices.content: The base areas are not the same.
It isn’t true that corresponding cross-sections have the same area.
The heights are not the same.

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the following two prisms.



[[☃ image 1]]

Mia tried to use Cavalieri's principle to show that the two figures have the same volume.

> *"The base areas are the same. Therefore, corresponding cross-sections have the same area. So the volumes must be the same."*

**What is the first mistake Mia made?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can use Cavalieri's principle to show that two figures have the same volume if we establish:

- Their base areas are the same.
- They have the same cross-sectional area at any given point above their bases.
- Their heights are the same.

Let's go through Mia's work part by part and find the first mistake that she made.

###Base areas

> *"The base areas are the same."*

$\begin{align}
\text{area}_\text{A} &= \dfrac12 bh
\\\\
&=\dfrac12 \cdot 6 \cdot 7
\\\\
&=21
\\\\
\text{area}_\text{B} &= \dfrac12bh
\\\\
&=\dfrac12 \cdot 7 \cdot 6
\\\\
&=21
\end{align}$

Mia is correct here.

###Cross-sectional areas

> *"...corresponding cross-sections have the same area."*


[[☃ image 1]]

In each figure, the cross-sectional area is the base area. We know the base areas are the same, and this area stays the same at each height, so Mia is correct here.

###Heights

Mia didn't say anything about the heights being the same. Cavalieri's principle relies on this, since each figure needs to have the same number of layers when it comes to their cross-sections.
Mia did not establish that heights are the same.

options.alt: A triangular prism and an oblique triangular prism that leans to the right at the top. The triangular prism has a small base of six units, a small height of seven units, and a prism height of fourteen units. The base of the oblique triangular prism has a base of seven units and a height of six units. One of the slanted edges of the oblique triangular prism has a length of fifteen units, but the oblique prism has a height of fourteen units.

choices.content: The base areas are not the same.
It isn’t true that corresponding cross-sections have the same area.
She did not establish that heights are the same.

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the following prisms, whose bases are both squares.



[[☃ image 1]]

Naomi tried to use Cavalieri's principle to show that the two figures have the same volume.

> *"The base areas are the same. Therefore, corresponding cross-sections have the same area. So the volumes must be the same."*

**What is the first mistake Naomi made?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can use Cavalieri's principle to show that two figures have the same volume if we establish:

- Their base areas are the same.
- They have the same cross-sectional area at any given point above their bases.
- Their heights are the same.

Let's go through Naomi's work part by part and find the first mistake that she made.

###Base areas

> *"The base areas are the same."*

The prisms have congruent bases — a square whose sides are all $8$ units long.

Naomi is correct here.

###Cross-sectional areas

> *"...corresponding cross-sections have the same area."*


[[☃ image 1]]

In each figure, the cross-sectional area is the base area. We know the base areas are the same, and this area stays the same at each height, so Naomi is correct here.

###Heights

Naomi didn't say anything about the heights being the same. Cavalieri's principle relies on this, since each figure needs to have the same number of layers when it comes to their cross-sections.
Naomi did not establish that heights are the same.

options.alt: A rectangular prism has a square base with a side length of eight units and a height of twelve units. An oblique rectangular prism has a square base with a side length of eight units and has a slant height of fifteen units and a vertical height of twelve units.

choices.content: The base areas are not the same.
It isn’t true that corresponding cross-sections have the same area.
She did not establish that heights are the same.

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the following rectangular-based and triangular-based prisms.



[[☃ image 1]]

Tim tried to use Cavalieri's principle to show that the two figures have the same volume.

> *"The base areas are the same. Therefore, corresponding cross-sections have the same area. So the volumes must be the same."*

**What is the first mistake Tim made?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can use Cavalieri's principle to show that two figures have the same volume if we establish:

> *"The base areas are the same."*

\text{area}_\text{rectangle} &= bh
